data.title: As a developer, a confirmed large refactor is reviewed by haiku agents checking pattern conformance and an opus agent inspecting anomalous files
data.description:
## Description

**What**: Implement the confirmed-refactor branch of REVIEW-WORKFLOW-HUGE.md. Dispatch haiku batch agents to check conformance of non-sampled files against the extracted pattern (defined in S2's contract), collect the anomalous file list, then dispatch the code-reviewer-refactor-anomaly opus reviewer for flagged files. The anomaly reviewer's output must be schema-compatible with record-review.sh validation so the pre-commit review gate can accept it.

**Why**: Delivers the primary value of this epic — efficient, reliable review for large refactors without paying the cost of full opus review on every file. Haiku handles the bulk mechanical conformance check; opus focuses only on files that deviate.

**Scope**:
- IN: haiku batch agent grouping strategy (respects orchestration.max_agents cap), per-file conformance check against extracted pattern, anomalous file collection, new code-reviewer-refactor-anomaly.md agent file in plugins/dso/agents/, agent dispatched inline by REVIEW-WORKFLOW-HUGE.md (same inline dispatch pattern as REVIEW-WORKFLOW.md deep-tier agents — not via agent-routing.conf), structured findings schema per-file (dimension: pattern_conformance | behavioral_drift | callsite_completeness; severity: critical | important | minor; description), review passing = no critical findings, anomaly findings schema compatible with record-review.sh (either mapping to standard 5-dimension categories or extending validate-review-output.sh with a refactor-anomaly prompt-id)
- OUT: fallback path (Story S4), configuration docs (Story S6)

## Done Definitions

- When this story is complete, haiku batch agents are dispatched in groups that respect orchestration.max_agents, with each agent receiving the extracted pattern (per the contract defined in S2) and checking a slice of the non-sampled files for conformance
  ← Satisfies: "Confirmed refactors proceed through haiku batch agents + a new refactor-anomaly opus reviewer that checks only flagged anomalous files"
- When this story is complete, the refactor-anomaly opus reviewer receives the list of anomalous files and emits a structured findings block with one entry per file carrying dimension label (pattern_conformance | behavioral_drift | callsite_completeness), severity (critical | important | minor), and a description; the review is marked passing when no critical findings are present
  ← Satisfies: "The refactor-anomaly opus reviewer emits a structured findings block with one entry per anomalous file, each carrying a dimension label, severity, and description; the review is considered passing when no critical findings are present"
- When this story is complete, a new code-reviewer-refactor-anomaly.md agent file exists in plugins/dso/agents/ and is dispatched inline by REVIEW-WORKFLOW-HUGE.md; the agent's output is schema-compatible with record-review.sh validation (either through dimension-to-category mapping or a new refactor-anomaly validator prompt-id in validate-review-output.sh)
  ← Satisfies: "The refactor-anomaly opus reviewer emits a structured findings block" (schema compatibility ensures the commit gate can use the findings)
- When this story is complete, unit tests are written and passing for all new or modified logic

## Considerations

- [Reliability] Anomaly reviewer timeout with many flagged files — define a maximum flagged-file count or timeout handling before dispatching; consider batching anomalous files if the count exceeds a threshold
- [Testing] Haiku batch grouping algorithm must be unit-testable without dispatching live agents — extract grouping logic so it can be tested with a mocked file list and max_agents value
- [Maintainability] Haiku batch agent grouping strategy should be consistent with existing multi-agent batch patterns in the codebase (e.g., how sprint.sh batches story agents)
- [Architecture] The anomaly reviewer's schema bridge with record-review.sh is the most implementation-sensitive decision in this story — the approach (map vs. extend) must be decided at implementation-plan time and documented

## Escalation Policy

**Escalation policy**: Proceed unless a significant assumption is required to continue — one that could send the implementation in the wrong direction. Escalate only when genuinely blocked without a reasonable inference. Document all assumptions made without escalating.
